source.messages:
Tomáš Novák: coming out of gameday + the rehearsal threads, we need to decide what runbook edits are *blocking* before we expand automation to more routes/models. can we align here so we don’t ship more confusion to oncall?

Rafael Mendes: +1. oncall feedback was basically “runbooks don’t match what the controller actually does now”. we should pick a minimal set that must be correct before we flip any more tenants.

Miles Thornton: agree. I’d separate into (A) correctness/safety (blocking), (B) usability (soon), (C) nice-to-have.

Kira Thompson: I can take edits but need crisp scope. right now we have 3 docs that point at each other and disagree on who approves what.

Tomáš Novák: exactly. proposed runbooks in scope for this thread:
- routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook (controller ops)
- hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook (oncall procedure)
- gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure (dedicated + infra)

Mina Haddad: add routing policy “what changes when” too. oncall keeps asking “is it DNS? is it policy engine?”

Allison Grant: and dashboards spec needs a “during event: where do i look first” section. but we can probably link it vs duplicating.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s list the blockers.

Miles Thornton: Blocker #1: single decision tree for “automation allowed vs manual” + required corroboration. right now Hosted API runbook says “if 5xx > X then failover” which is not true anymore (needs reachability or burn corroboration per ADR-042).

Tomáš Novák: yes. oncall was ready to failover on a single noisy 5xx spike in eu-west. need “DO NOT FAILOVER on isolated 5xx if perf-canary reachability is green + control plane healthy” spelled out.

Victor Alvarez: +1. add explicit network checks before blaming region: perf-canary cross-region probe panel + cloud provider status page link. Also note that DNS TTL changes mean clients may not move instantly.

Peter Holtz: Blocker #2: circuit breaker / retry budget guidance during failover. we saw thundering herd due to clients retrying immediately on 307/429. runbook should say: keep gateway retry budget low, prefer `retry-after`, and when to enable load shedding mode.

Rafael Mendes: yep. we need a “set expectations for error shape” section: 429 + Retry-After during overload is expected; it’s safer than timeouts.

Kira Thompson: i can add a “Customer impact expectations” box: what users will see (streaming disconnects, redirect, brief 429). But need final wording from runtime/gateway folks.

Devon Price: Blocker #3: incident-bot + comms steps. During the test, we weren’t sure when to post internal update vs status page note. runbook should say who triggers the comms playbook + what’s optional for planned tests.

Tomáš Novák: +1. also: who owns the “go/no-go” and who can hit the big red disable automation.

Rafael Mendes: for prod: oncall SRE (primary) can disable automation, Miles (or routing oncall) can override policy flips. Need this written.

Miles Thornton: Blocker #4: rollback semantics. oncall didn’t know whether rollback was automatic or manual and what the hold-down timer is. we need explicit “after failover: minimum hold-down N minutes; rollback only if X/Y/Z signals recover AND traffic is stable” so we don’t flap.

Mina Haddad: please also include the per-tenant safety limits (max flips/hour). oncall asked “why won’t it flip again?” and assumed it was broken.

Allison Grant: Blocker #5: dashboard links + “what good looks like” per signal. for each step in runbook, link the panel:
- reachability (perf-canary)
- 5xx burn + latency burn (SLO burn)
- capacity headroom
- policy flip/audit events
And add 1-2 bullets on interpreting each.

Kira Thompson: can we just hardcode the canonical dashboard URLs? or do we need environment-specific placeholders?

Allison Grant: put canonical + a note: “if in staging, switch env filter to staging-*”. we can keep it simple.

Victor Alvarez: also add the DNS/anycast constraints section as “FYI”: TTL 60s doesn’t mean all clients re-resolve. some SDKs cache longer.

Tomáš Novák: ok, summarizing blockers so far:
1) Decision tree + trigger corroboration + “do not failover” cases (ADR-042 aligned)
2) Circuit breaker / retry budget / load shedding mode + expected error shapes
3) Ownership + approvals + comms hooks (incident-bot/status)
4) Rollback semantics + hold-down timers + anti-flap / safety limits
5) Dashboards: direct links + interpretation guidance
Anything else truly blocking?

Rafael Mendes: Dedicated: blocker #6 is drain sequencing. The dedicated drain runbook currently starts with “drain pool” but doesn’t say “confirm routing has shifted tenant traffic” vs “drain will cause 5xx”. needs a safe order.

Miles Thornton: yes: “route -> verify -> drain -> verify -> scale”. plus explicit abort conditions.

Peter Holtz: and make it clear when drain automation is used vs manual commands. if it’s feature-flagged, say so.

Mina Haddad: also: add a tiny glossary. “region impaired” vs “partial degradation” vs “control plane degraded”. we had people talking past each other.

Tomáš Novák: glossary is nice, but is it blocking?

Rafael Mendes: borderline, but I’d include a 5-line definitions section at top of Hosted API runbook. it reduces mistakes.

Allison Grant: +1. definitions reduce wrong paging too.

Kira Thompson: ok, I’ll include a short Definitions section.

Devon Price: one more: audit trail. enterprise asks “show me what happened” (SUP-25444-ish). runbook should say where audit events live and how to export links for support.

Rafael Mendes: good call. put as “Post-event: collect artifacts” checklist.

Tomáš Novák: so priorities / order of operations:
P0 (must land before enabling automation for additional routes/models): blockers 1-6 + definitions + audit trail pointers.
P1: nicer troubleshooting playbooks (e.g., “controller says NO_ACTION, now what?”) + deeper client SDK guidance.

Miles Thornton: works. assign owners?

Kira Thompson: I can draft the runbook edits if I get reviewers and source of truth for hold-down timers + safety limits.

Mina Haddad: i’ll provide the routing/policy details (hold-down, anti-flap, safety limits) + what toggles exist.

Peter Holtz: i’ll write the gateway/load shedding / retry budget section + expected 429/503/307 behavior.

Allison Grant: i’ll drop the canonical dashboard links + quick “reading signals” bullets.

Devon Price: I’ll add comms + incident-bot steps + what gets auto-posted vs manual.

Victor Alvarez: I’ll add the network checks and DNS caveats section + links to the networking notes.

Rafael Mendes: i’ll own final review for “oncall correctness” + publish checklist. target: edits merged by EOW so we can unblock next rollout wave.

Tomáš Novák: perfect. can we agree: no expansion to more routes/models until the P0 runbooks are updated and reviewed by SRE + routing + infra?

Rafael Mendes: yes. :thumbsup:

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: also let’s add a short “runbook version” note in the rollout checklist so we can confirm prod oncall is looking at the right doc.

Kira Thompson: ack. i’ll start a doc PR / change log section so we can point to what changed after gameday.

Allison Grant: please ping me when you want dashboard links; i’ll make sure they’re stable + include the annotation panel callout.

Tomáš Novák: thanks all. i’ll post a mini checklist in-thread once Kira has the draft so reviewers know what to look for.

source.thread:
Tomáš Novák: Rehearsal debrief (on-call POV) — we’ve got some runbook gaps that slowed us down / caused confusion. Dropping here so we can turn into concrete edits.

Tomáš Novák: Main pain points:
1) Missing steps before approving automation (esp. in prod).
2) Ownership boundaries weren’t obvious (routing vs control plane vs infra vs on-call).
3) Dashboards/queries referenced verbally but not linked anywhere.

Tomáš Novák: Specific gaps I hit (Hosted API regional failover runbook):
- No explicit “is this a region-level event vs partial degradation?” decision tree.
- Doesn’t say “check region health service output first” (and which dashboard panel).
- No step for confirming cross-region reachability probes are failing/passing.
- No mention of capacity headroom check in target region before flipping.
- Rollback section is too generic: doesn’t include hold-down timer behavior / anti-flap constraints.

Tomáš Novák: Specific gaps (routing-policy failover controller runbook):
- Dry-run mode steps are missing (what command to run + what output we expect).
- Doesn’t say where audit events show up (or how to correlate to incident timeline).
- No “stop the bleeding” section for noisy triggers (e.g., disable trigger source X, keep reachability authoritative).

Tomáš Novák: Confusing ownership:
- Who approves the policy flip in prod? On-call SRE vs routing on-call vs incident commander.
- Dedicated pool drain: is that infra on-call (Irene’s team) or SRE initiating via controller workflow?
- If region health signals are missing (e.g., perf-canary lagging), who pages whom?

Tomáš Novák: Small but painful: we couldn’t find the exact dashboards quickly. We ended up pasting random Grafana links in-thread. Runbook should have a “Links” section at top.

Rafael Mendes: :eyes: thanks. This matches what I heard during the rehearsal. Can you paste the exact spots where you got stuck / what you expected to see?

Tomáš Novák: Yep.
- Hosted runbook “Verify impairment” section: expected it to tell me *which* evidence is required for automated failover (signals + thresholds).
- Controller runbook: expected a “dry-run first, then request approval” flow, including what counts as “safe to execute”.

Miles Thornton: Agree on decision tree. We’ve been relying on tribal knowledge: reachability > 5xx burn > latency burn > capacity. We should encode that as a step-by-step checklist in the on-call runbook.

Miles Thornton: On approvals: current intended model for prod is “automation recommends, on-call SRE acks” (until we remove approval hooks). That needs to be spelled out, including what to do if routing on-call disagrees.

Mina Haddad: +1. Also add “how to identify *routing policy* changes in the console / config” so on-call isn’t guessing whether the controller actually executed.

Allison Grant: On dashboards: I can add a pinned “Failover Signals” folder + stable links (no ad-hoc queries). But runbook should reference:
- Region health summary panel (per region)
- Reachability probe success rate
- 5xx burn (multi-window)
- Tail latency burn (p95/p99)
- Capacity headroom + queue depth
- Policy flips + audit events

Allison Grant: We should also note “if dashboards lack annotations, check audit log stream” — that came up multiple times.

Devon Price: Incident-bot now posts a summary on failover recommend/execute/rollback (PR merged last week). Runbooks don’t mention it.

Devon Price: Example message includes links to dashboards + the audit event id. We should add “look for incident-bot summary in #incidents / your incident channel” as a step.

Peter Holtz: From gateway/circuit breaker side: runbook currently says “reduce retries” but doesn’t specify what knobs are safe during failover.

Peter Holtz: Suggest we add a short section:
- confirm current retry budget config
- during failover: enforce `Retry-After` semantics / avoid client retry storms
- confirm timeouts aren’t too aggressive causing false 5xx burn

Victor Alvarez: Networking note: please don’t put DNS TTL changes as an on-call action unless we explicitly want that. It’s a change-managed thing and can make things worse if done mid-incident.

Victor Alvarez: But we *should* add “if reachability probes fail from multiple vantage points, escalate to Network (Victor)” and link to the networking notes doc.

Kira Thompson: I can take the first pass at editing both runbooks. Can someone drop the canonical Confluence links (and any “must match ADR” requirements) so I don’t miss details?

Rafael Mendes: Links:
- Hosted API regional failover runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Hosted+API+Regional+Failover
- Failover controller runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Routing+Policy+Failover+Controller

Rafael Mendes: Also relevant ADRs:
- ADR-042 trigger hierarchy
- ADR-043 rollback semantics

Miles Thornton: For “must match ADR”: runbook should reflect:
- corroboration requirements (reachability + burn rate etc)
- dampening/hold-down timers (and how they affect rollback)
- anti-flap rate limits

Tomáš Novák: Action items I propose (feel free to amend):
[ ] Add “Quick links” section at top of both runbooks (dashboards, audit log, incident-bot docs)
[ ] Add decision tree: region event vs partial degradation (incl. “do NOT fail over” cases)
[ ] Add dry-run mode procedure with acceptance criteria (what output must show)
[ ] Add explicit roles: who is IC, who acks automation, who executes dedicated drain
[ ] Add pre-flight checklist: target region capacity + error budget burn + reachability
[ ] Add rollback checklist + how to interpret hold-down timer behavior
[ ] Add “missing signal stream” troubleshooting + paging guidance

Allison Grant: Add one more:
[ ] Ensure dashboards have automation-driven annotations for recommend/execute/rollback (we missed this in rehearsal)

Devon Price: +1, and:
[ ] Document incident-bot message format + where it posts

Peter Holtz: Add:
[ ] Gateway config “safe knobs” cheat sheet (timeouts/retries/circuit breaker states) + what not to touch

Victor Alvarez: Add:
[ ] Network escalation triggers + “don’t touch DNS mid-incident” note

Kira Thompson: Sounds good. I’ll draft edits today and tag @Tomáš @Miles @Rafael for review. Target: PR to Confluence by EOD tomorrow.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: I’ll make sure we align with the game day checklist too. If we can’t follow the runbook during rehearsal, we won’t follow it during a real region event.

Tomáš Novák: Thank you. I’ll also add a short section from “on-call narrative” perspective: what I actually do in the first 5 minutes.

Miles Thornton: Please include a “what success looks like” box (expected failover time, acceptable error/latency blip, when to rollback).

Allison Grant: I’ll get stable dashboard URLs into the runbook draft (no more “search grafana for X”). Will post them here once pinned.

Devon Price: I’ll update incident-bot doc + provide a sample message snippet for Kira.

Rafael Mendes: Cool. Let’s track these in ENG-2423 (runbook refresh) and link back here.

Tomáš Novák: Done. Adding note there now. :writing_hand:

Rafael Mendes: (also: if anyone remembers other “WTF moments” from rehearsal, reply in-thread so we capture them all)

Tomáš Novák: One more: we didn’t have a clean instruction for “pause automation” vs “disable automation” (feature flag vs controller toggle). We need exact wording + command to avoid half-disabled states.

Miles Thornton: Good catch. Add explicit: “set controller mode = observe-only (dry-run)” vs “disable controller deployment” and when each is appropriate.

Kira Thompson: Got it. Will add a “Modes of operation” section with guardrails + who can change it in prod.

Allison Grant: FYI I found we have two nearly-identical dashboards with different label sets. I’ll deprecate one and update runbook links so we stop bouncing between them.

Rafael Mendes: :pray: please. That cost us like 10 minutes.

Tomáš Novák: +1. That was the main reason I’m pushing for “quick links” section.

Rafael Mendes: Closing loop: Kira owns edits, Allison owns dashboard links/annotations, Devon owns incident-bot docs, Peter adds gateway cheat sheet notes, Victor provides network escalation snippet. I’ll review + merge.

Kira Thompson: ack :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Heads up: we’re ~1 week out from shadow traffic quality metrics beta (Dedicated customers). Need to lock oncall readiness items + owners. Dropping checklist here — please confirm status/ETA.

Sean Gallagher: ✅ Must-have before we flip beta flag on any external tenant:
- Dashboards: quality deltas, scoring lag/backlog, shadow target error rate, ingestion lag, cardinality guardrails
- Alerts: paging vs ticket thresholds (esp. “shadow path must be fail-open”)
- Runbooks: enable/disable shadowing, rollback steps, queue backlog playbook, telemetry lag playbook
- Kill switches: per-tenant + global; safe defaults
- Escalation: named humans for routing/policy, telemetry, scoring workers, console

Talia Benson: Adding program mgmt view: can we attach this to the beta go/no-go checklist? Also need a single “where to look first” for primary oncall.

Allison Grant: Dashboards: I can own. We have draft panels in observability-pack PR but need to verify card-safe dims. Proposed dashboards:
1) Shadow Traffic Overview (per env): sampled QPS, mirror success rate, shadow timeout %, p50/p99 overhead on user path
2) Quality Delta Summary: metric deltas w/ confidence bands, sample size, segment filter limited to route/model/region/tier
3) Scoring Pipeline Health: queue depth, oldest msg age, worker error rate, rubric runner latency, retries
4) Telemetry/Ingestion: event ingest lag, dropped events, redaction failures

Allison Grant: I’ll paste links once PR merges. ETA EOD tomorrow for a stable version.

Logan Wright: Telemetry side: we have the “shadow_pair” events in staging now. Need to finalize alerting on ingestion lag + dropped events. Current plan:
- page if ingest lag > 10m for 15m (beta tenants only)
- ticket if lag > 2m for 30m
- page if redaction failure > 0.5% for 10m (could indicate bad config)

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also adding metric for “shadow_event_shed_count” when circuit breaker opens.

Dylan Brooks: Console oncall: we’ll have feature flagged UI. Failure mode is “no data” / stale results. We should add banner in UI when scoring lag > threshold (so support doesn’t file 20 tickets). I can add a minimal banner behind flag.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Can we key the banner off a single health endpoint rather than sprinkling queries?

Dylan Brooks: Yep. We can call console-backend `/internal/shadow/health` and just show status + “last scored at …”. Need owner for that endpoint.

Logan Wright: Telemetry can expose “last_seen_scored_event_ts” per tenant, but console backend can just query aggregates table. I’ll sync w/ Chloe/Jada unless someone already doing it.

Ethan Park: Control plane kill switches: we have
- global FF: `shadow_traffic_enabled`
- per-tenant override: `shadow_traffic_tenant_allowlist`
- per-route policy: `shadow_policy.enabled=false`
We should document precedence + how to flip quickly.

Sean Gallagher: Need explicit “break glass” procedure: if oncall sees user-path p99 regression, we nuke shadowing immediately. What’s the lowest-latency control? Feature flag service?

Ethan Park: Fastest is global FF in LaunchDarkly (prop ~30s). Per-tenant allowlist also quick. Route policy change goes through config push pipeline (minutes).

Mina Haddad: Routing has circuit breaker too: if shadow target latency > threshold or error rate spikes, we stop mirroring automatically. But kill switch still needed.

Selene Huang: For alerts: please do NOT page on “quality regression” thresholds in beta until we’ve calibrated. We should page only on pipeline health / user path SLO risk.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Quality regression alerts should be advisory (ticket + console highlight), not a page.

Allison Grant: Proposed paging policy for beta (please react):
P0 Page:
- User-path overhead attributable to shadowing (mirror enqueue time) p99 > 5ms for 10m OR any coupling causing request errors
- Circuit breaker open % > 20% for 10m (indicates thrash) + user-path overhead > 0
- Scoring backlog oldest age > 60m for 15m (only if beta tenants active)
P1 Ticket:
- Ingestion lag > 2m for 30m
- Worker error rate > 2% for 30m
- Redaction failures > 0.5% for 10m

Allison Grant: Dims kept small: `tenant_tier`, `region`, `route_id_bucketed`, `shadow_variant_id`, `rubric_version`. No raw `prompt_id`.

Nadia Rahman: :thumbsup: on dims. Please keep `shadow_variant_id` as Redwood ID only (no customer-supplied strings).

Marcus Lin: Security note: runbook should include “do not ask customer to send prompts in Slack”. If debugging needs examples, use the “sampled examples” endpoint w/ RBAC + audit log. Also confirm audit logging is on for enable/disable actions.

Ethan Park: Audit logs landed for experiment changes + policy changes. We should link the query in runbook (what to search).

Talia Benson: Can someone assign owners + ETAs per item? We need this for the beta readiness review with Priya.

Sean Gallagher: Owners / ETAs proposal:
- Dashboards + alert rules: Allison (ETA tomorrow EOD)
- Telemetry ingestion + lag alerts: Logan/Nadia (ETA Thu)
- Control plane kill switch + precedence doc: Ethan (ETA Thu)
- Routing circuit breaker thresholds doc: Mina (ETA Thu)
- Scoring pipeline runbook + autoscale knobs: Jonas (ETA Fri)
- Console banner + health endpoint: Dylan + Logan (ETA Fri)
- Security checklist addendum + escalation contacts: Marcus + Sean (ETA Fri)
React if you agree or comment.

OncallBot: Reminder set by Sean Gallagher: “Shadow traffic beta readiness check-in” at 2026-01-14 10:00 PT.

Jonas Weber: Scoring runbook: I’ll draft. Includes:
- how to tell if backlog is “expected” (sampling ramp) vs broken
- how to pause scoring workers without impacting user path
- how to reprocess failed rubric jobs
- where rubric versions are pinned
ETA Fri noon.

Selene Huang: Can we add a quick note in runbook on interpreting small sample sizes? Support keeps screenshotting deltas with n=20.

Jonas Weber: Yep. I’ll add “minimum n” guidance + confidence interval callout.

Sean Gallagher: Escalation contacts (beta period):
- Primary oncall: SRE oncall (pager)
- Routing/data plane: Mina Haddad / Vikram Singh
- Control plane/policy: Ethan Park / Benji Okafor
- Telemetry/ingestion: Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman
- Scoring/rubrics: Jonas Weber / Selene Huang
- Console: Dylan Brooks
- Security: Marcus Lin
If any of these are wrong, please correct.

Dylan Brooks: Console contact is fine. Also looping in Chloe/Jada for backend if needed, but I’m happy as first stop.

Allison Grant: I’ll thread links here once the dashboards PR merges + alert rules are deployed to staging.

Logan Wright: One more: we should add “beta tenant list” to dashboards as a filter/preset so we don’t accidentally page for internal dogfood. Where’s the source of truth?

Talia Benson: Beta tenant list is in the beta plan doc; I’ll paste IDs in #eng-oncall once finalized. For now: use tenant label `beta_shadow=true`.

Nadia Rahman: We have that label already, emitted from control plane config.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Final ask: please sanity check that all pages are “pipeline health / user path risk” only. No paging on “quality delta” for now. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: :thumbsup:

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup:

Jonas Weber: :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Owen Phillips: Heads up, we keep bouncing w/ customers on "can you share logs" and it takes forever. Can we standardize what we ask for when SDK calls fail (timeouts/429/stream disconnect)? Specifically request_id + SDK version. What’s the best set of questions for oncall/support?

Dev Patel: +1. We need a crisp macro. Right now folks ask for "request id" but customers don’t know where to find it, esp in streaming.

Ibrahim Khan: For parity work we’re making `request_id` show up in typed errors, but until everyone upgrades we should ask for BOTH:
- request_id (from error object if present)
- OR the response header `x-request-id` if they can capture it
Also ask for SDK language + exact version + config (retries/timeouts).

Allison Grant: Agree. For oncall triage, minimum viable dataset:
1) request_id (or multiple, if it’s retrying)
2) timestamp (with timezone) + region (if they know)
3) endpoint + model + streaming vs non-streaming
4) SDK name/version + runtime (Python/Node/Go version)
5) retry config: max_attempts, backoff, whether they respect Retry-After
6) timeout config (overall/connect/read if applicable)
If it’s streaming: do they see partial tokens? where does it stop? any proxy?

Dev Patel: Can we give them copy/paste instructions to get SDK version quickly?

Ibrahim Khan: Yup. Something like:
- Python: `python -c "import redwood; print(redwood.__version__)"`
- Node: `npm ls @redwood-ai/sdk` (or `pnpm why ...`)
- Go: `go list -m all | grep redwood` (or whatever module path is)
We should keep it generic in the macro and link to docs.

Monica Patel: Let’s turn this into a playbook update. We already have INT-1182 open for request_id visibility; we can also update the oncall checklist + support macro now.
Action: I’ll ask Dev + Owen to propose wording, we’ll review in DevEx.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: please also ask which SDK major/minor they’re on (pre- vs post-standardization). We’re going to have a wave of mixed behavior for a bit.
Maybe add: “Are you on Python >= X / TS >= Y / Go >= Z?” (we can fill versions once the stable tags are out).

Nadia Rahman: Also worth asking for retry_count if they have logs. We’re adding standardized fields soon but today customers can log attempts themselves.
Suggested question: “Do you see multiple attempts for the same user action? If yes, approx how many?”

Marcus Lin: Security reminder for the macro: explicitly say “Do NOT send API keys/bearer tokens.”
If they paste headers, ask them to redact `Authorization`.
If we recommend debug logging, ensure it’s clear Redwood SDKs redact secrets but customer app logs might not.

Dev Patel: Draft macro v0 (feedback welcome):
"To help us investigate, please reply with:
1) Your SDK language + version (and your runtime version, e.g. Node 20 / Python 3.11 / Go 1.22)
2) request_id for a failed call (from the error message/object, or from the `x-request-id` response header). If it retried, include *all* request_ids you have.
3) Approx timestamp + timezone
4) Endpoint + model + whether streaming was enabled
5) Your retry + timeout settings (max attempts, any custom backoff, timeout values)
6) If streaming: did you receive partial output? any corporate proxy/VPN?
Please do not include API keys or Authorization headers."

Allison Grant: Looks good. One more thing: ask if they’re using our OpenAI-compat endpoint vs native, since error shapes differed historically.

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Add a line: “Which endpoint style are you using: Redwood native SDK calls vs OpenAI-compatible base_url?”

Owen Phillips: This is great. I’ll update the oncall runbook doc + the Zendesk macro and link it in #support.
Also going to add a section "How to find request_id" with examples per SDK once the new errors land.

Monica Patel: Please file a small ticket under the parity epic for the macro + runbook update so it doesn’t get lost. Tag me + Dev.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: When you add versions, ping #eng-releases so we keep them aligned with the actual ship vehicles.

Dev Patel: Will do. I’ll open INT-1199 follow-up w/ the macro text + request_id instructions and assign to Owen/me.

Owen Phillips: Thanks all. This should cut the back-and-forth a lot during incidents.

source.text:
Rafael Mendes: putting together oncall procedures for KV compaction canary. need crisp guidance: when to flip kill-switch vs just reduce budgets, what dashboards to look at, and who to pull in. pls drop thoughts here.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should make it very binary for oncall: “is compaction likely the cause” -> “do X”. last canary we spent 20 min debating.

Allison Grant: dashboards first. for compaction specifically we should require checking:
- Hosted API Tail Latency (p95/p99) by route/model
- KV allocator health: fragmentation_ratio, free_list_depth, largest_contig_block_bytes
- compaction: bytes_moved_total, compaction_step_time_ms, compaction_budget_hit_count
- eviction: kv_evictions_total (by reason + tier), pinned_eviction_failures
- GPU: mem_used, allocator_oom_count, request_shed_count

Rafael Mendes: do we have canonical links yet or should I just put the grafana search keywords?

Allison Grant: links are fine if stable. otherwise “search: kv.fragmentation_ratio” etc. (and include metric names exactly)

Benji Okafor: kill-switch knobs we have in runtime config (today):
- runtime.kv.compaction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick
- runtime.kv.compaction.max_bytes_per_tick
- runtime.kv.eviction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min
plus a “safe mode” we discussed: set budget to 0 instead of full disable (lets compaction state machine drain without moving pages). not sure if we landed it.

Samir Qureshi: safe mode exists in latest canary build: if enabled=false, relocation stops immediately but leaves any in-flight relocation to finish current step (we guard with refcounts). if budget_ms=0 but enabled=true, it keeps measuring fragmentation + will schedule zero move work (useful to confirm correlation w/o fully toggling).

Sean Gallagher: oncall guidance should be:
1) first response: reduce compaction budget to 0 (NOT full disable) if p99 spikes and compaction_step_time_ms correlates.
2) if p99 still bad, or we see allocator_oom_count climbing, disable compaction + disable eviction (eviction can also cause churn/extra copies)
3) if OOM / shedding: disable compaction immediately, then page runtime.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: in OOM scenario you might want eviction ON (to prevent hard OOM) but compaction OFF (to reduce copy overhead). depends on what’s triggering. if the OOM is from fragmentation, eviction might *help* while we back off compaction.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “when to keep eviction on” as a rule?

Omar El-Sayed: suggestion:
- If we see rising allocator_oom_count OR request_shed_count due to KV allocation failures -> keep eviction enabled, cap evictions_per_min to avoid thrash.
- If we see quality issues / session resets / elevated “sequence_aborted” (not sure metric name) -> consider eviction off, and we may need runtime to look.

Allison Grant: metrics we have for eviction impact:
- kv.eviction.abort_sequence_total
- kv.eviction.pinned_skip_total
- kv.eviction.prefix_cache_drop_total
not perfect but gives signal.

Sean Gallagher: threshold triggers: can we put numbers? e.g.
- if p99 decode latency +20% for >10m in canary cohort AND compaction bytes moved > baseline by 2x
- or compaction_step_time_ms > 5ms p95 -> action

Paula Mendes: rollout automation is already watching p99 + error rate, but oncall doc should say what triggers manual rollback/disable:
- p99 > 1.25x baseline for 10m (canary)
- request_shed_rate > 0.5% for 5m
- allocator_oom_count > 0 sustained
If any of those + compaction metrics are “active”, kill-switch is authorized.

Mateo Alvarez: authority: SRE/eng-oncall can flip kill-switch in an incident without runtime approval. just notify #eng-runtime and open incident channel. if not an incident, please loop Omar/Samir first.

Rafael Mendes: ok. I’ll put that explicitly: “if paging incident, flip first, ask questions after.”

Benji Okafor: config propagation latency: expect 30-90s to hit all hosts in a pool; dedicated can be slower if control plane is busy. oncall should watch the “config applied” metric (we emit runtime.config.last_applied_ts).

Samir Qureshi: also note: disabling compaction doesn’t reclaim already-fragmented layout immediately. improvement should be “no further spikes from moving pages”. fragmentation_ratio may stay high until churn evens out.

Sean Gallagher: escalation path draft:
- If p99 spike + compaction correlated: page Runtime KV (Omar), then Allocator (Samir)
- If correctness suspicion (bad outputs / crashes in relocation): page Noah Patel
- If GPU copy kernel hot / high SM util during compaction: page Grace Kim (kernels)
- For rollback help / flag propagation issues: page Benji

Rafael Mendes: yes. also add “SRE primary owns incident + comms; runtime owns mitigation + followup PR.”

Allison Grant: add a “what dashboards to screenshot” section for incident notes:
- p99 chart with cohort filter
- fragmentation_ratio + bytes_moved overlay
- eviction rate
- allocator_oom_count + shed

Sean Gallagher: command snippets? makes it easier.

Benji Okafor: examples (internal):
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick --value 0`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.enabled --value false`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min --value 60`
(+ include “verify” command: `rwdctl config get ...`)

Omar El-Sayed: also add “verify in runtime logs”: look for `kv_compaction state=disabled reason=operator` and `compaction_budget_ms=0` lines. ppl trust logs more than dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. I’ll draft runbook section:
- Triage checklist (5 min)
- Decision tree for budget=0 vs disable vs eviction changes
- Verification steps (dashboard + logs + config applied timestamp)
- Escalations + who can flip

Incident Bot: Reminder: If this is customer-impacting, open an incident in #incidents and attach dashboards + config change log.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also: if we flip kill-switch, add a follow-up task to re-enable w/ smaller budget + canary only. don’t leave it off forever.

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll put a “post-mitigation” checklist: (1) file retro note, (2) revert to canary defaults next business day w/ runtime owner, (3) update thresholds if alerts were noisy.

Samir Qureshi: also note: disabling compaction doesn’t reclaim already-fragmented layout immediately. improvement should be “no further spikes from moving pages”. fragmentation_ratio may stay high until churn evens out.

Sean Gallagher: escalation path draft:
- If p99 spike + compaction correlated: page Runtime KV (Omar), then Allocator (Samir)
- If correctness suspicion (bad outputs / crashes in relocation): page Noah Patel
- If GPU copy kernel hot / high SM util during compaction: page Grace Kim (kernels)
- For rollback help / flag propagation issues: page Benji

Rafael Mendes: yes. also add “SRE primary owns incident + comms; runtime owns mitigation + followup PR.”

Allison Grant: add a “what dashboards to screenshot” section for incident notes:
- p99 chart with cohort filter
- fragmentation_ratio + bytes_moved overlay
- eviction rate
- allocator_oom_count + shed

Sean Gallagher: command snippets? makes it easier.

Benji Okafor: examples (internal):
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick --value 0`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.enabled --value false`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min --value 60`
(+ include “verify” command: `rwdctl config get ...`)

Omar El-Sayed: also add “verify in runtime logs”: look for `kv_compaction state=disabled reason=operator` and `compaction_budget_ms=0` lines. ppl trust logs more than dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. I’ll draft runbook section:
- Triage checklist (5 min)
- Decision tree for budget=0 vs disable vs eviction changes
- Verification steps (dashboard + logs + config applied timestamp)
- Escalations + who can flip

Incident Bot: Reminder: If this is customer-impacting, open an incident in #incidents and attach dashboards + config change log.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also: if we flip kill-switch, add a follow-up task to re-enable w/ smaller budget + canary only. don’t leave it off forever.

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll put a “post-mitigation” checklist: (1) file retro note, (2) revert to canary defaults next business day w/ runtime owner, (3) update thresholds if alerts were noisy.
text:
Rafael Mendes: putting together oncall procedures for KV compaction canary. need crisp guidance: when to flip kill-switch vs just reduce budgets, what dashboards to look at, and who to pull in. pls drop thoughts here.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should make it very binary for oncall: “is compaction likely the cause” -> “do X”. last canary we spent 20 min debating.

Allison Grant: dashboards first. for compaction specifically we should require checking:
- Hosted API Tail Latency (p95/p99) by route/model
- KV allocator health: fragmentation_ratio, free_list_depth, largest_contig_block_bytes
- compaction: bytes_moved_total, compaction_step_time_ms, compaction_budget_hit_count
- eviction: kv_evictions_total (by reason + tier), pinned_eviction_failures
- GPU: mem_used, allocator_oom_count, request_shed_count

Rafael Mendes: do we have canonical links yet or should I just put the grafana search keywords?

Allison Grant: links are fine if stable. otherwise “search: kv.fragmentation_ratio” etc. (and include metric names exactly)

Benji Okafor: kill-switch knobs we have in runtime config (today):
- runtime.kv.compaction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick
- runtime.kv.compaction.max_bytes_per_tick
- runtime.kv.eviction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min
plus a “safe mode” we discussed: set budget to 0 instead of full disable (lets compaction state machine drain without moving pages). not sure if we landed it.

Samir Qureshi: safe mode exists in latest canary build: if enabled=false, relocation stops immediately but leaves any in-flight relocation to finish current step (we guard with refcounts). if budget_ms=0 but enabled=true, it keeps measuring fragmentation + will schedule zero move work (useful to confirm correlation w/o fully toggling).

Sean Gallagher: oncall guidance should be:
1) first response: reduce compaction budget to 0 (NOT full disable) if p99 spikes and compaction_step_time_ms correlates.
2) if p99 still bad, or we see allocator_oom_count climbing, disable compaction + disable eviction (eviction can also cause churn/extra copies)
3) if OOM / shedding: disable compaction immediately, then page runtime.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: in OOM scenario you might want eviction ON (to prevent hard OOM) but compaction OFF (to reduce copy overhead). depends on what’s triggering. if the OOM is from fragmentation, eviction might *help* while we back off compaction.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “when to keep eviction on” as a rule?

Omar El-Sayed: suggestion:
- If we see rising allocator_oom_count OR request_shed_count due to KV allocation failures -> keep eviction enabled, cap evictions_per_min to avoid thrash.
- If we see quality issues / session resets / elevated “sequence_aborted” (not sure metric name) -> consider eviction off, and we may need runtime to look.

Allison Grant: metrics we have for eviction impact:
- kv.eviction.abort_sequence_total
- kv.eviction.pinned_skip_total
- kv.eviction.prefix_cache_drop_total
not perfect but gives signal.

Sean Gallagher: threshold triggers: can we put numbers? e.g.
- if p99 decode latency +20% for >10m in canary cohort AND compaction bytes moved > baseline by 2x
- or compaction_step_time_ms > 5ms p95 -> action

Paula Mendes: rollout automation is already watching p99 + error rate, but oncall doc should say what triggers manual rollback/disable:
- p99 > 1.25x baseline for 10m (canary)
- request_shed_rate > 0.5% for 5m
- allocator_oom_count > 0 sustained
If any of those + compaction metrics are “active”, kill-switch is authorized.

Mateo Alvarez: authority: SRE/eng-oncall can flip kill-switch in an incident without runtime approval. just notify #eng-runtime and open incident channel. if not an incident, please loop Omar/Samir first.

Rafael Mendes: ok. I’ll put that explicitly: “if paging incident, flip first, ask questions after.”

Benji Okafor: config propagation latency: expect 30-90s to hit all hosts in a pool; dedicated can be slower if control plane is busy. oncall should watch the “config applied” metric (we emit runtime.config.last_applied_ts).

Samir Qureshi: also note: disabling compaction doesn’t reclaim already-fragmented layout immediately. improvement should be “no further spikes from moving pages”. fragmentation_ratio may stay high until churn evens out.

Sean Gallagher: escalation path draft:
- If p99 spike + compaction correlated: page Runtime KV (Omar), then Allocator (Samir)
- If correctness suspicion (bad outputs / crashes in relocation): page Noah Patel
- If GPU copy kernel hot / high SM util during compaction: page Grace Kim (kernels)
- For rollback help / flag propagation issues: page Benji

Rafael Mendes: yes. also add “SRE primary owns incident + comms; runtime owns mitigation + followup PR.”

Allison Grant: add a “what dashboards to screenshot” section for incident notes:
- p99 chart with cohort filter
- fragmentation_ratio + bytes_moved overlay
- eviction rate
- allocator_oom_count + shed

Sean Gallagher: command snippets? makes it easier.

Benji Okafor: examples (internal):
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick --value 0`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.enabled --value false`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min --value 60`
(+ include “verify” command: `rwdctl config get ...`)

Omar El-Sayed: also add “verify in runtime logs”: look for `kv_compaction state=disabled reason=operator` and `compaction_budget_ms=0` lines. ppl trust logs more than dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. I’ll draft runbook section:

- Escalations + who can flip

source.messages:
Sean Gallagher: Drafting a quick triage playbook for profile-related regressions (hardware tuning profiles pack). Need: what toggles to flip (fast), what dashboards to check, what logs to collect. Pasting an outline here for review.

Sean Gallagher: Proposed structure:
1) Confirm scope (which pool/region, which GPU SKU, which runtime/profile version)
2) Identify regression class (latency p95/p99, throughput drop, OOM/restarts, error rates)
3) Immediate mitigation (disable profiles / switch to baseline / reduce batch / cap KV)
4) Evidence collection (metrics + logs + config dump)
5) Escalation + rollback criteria

Kira Thompson: +1. Also include “how to verify which profile is actually active” because we keep assuming H100-balanced but overrides change it.

Benji Okafor: Yep. With pr-22027 we now expose selected profile + override source. Oncall should hit the debug endpoint first, then decide whether to disable per-pool.

Aisha Bello: For dashboards: we have new panels in Observability pack segmenting latency by `runtime_profile` and `override_source`. I can drop the canonical links once the dashboard names are finalized.

Paula Mendes: Don’t forget: rollback thresholds need to be explicit enough that oncall can act without paging 6 people. We agreed last week on “p95 > +8% for 15 min OR error rate > 0.5% for 5 min” as default guardrails (unless SKU-specific). Put that in.

Sean Gallagher: Ok, filling in details. Here’s the “fast path” toggles list I’ve got — confirm naming.

Sean Gallagher: Fast mitigations (Hosted/Dedicated fleet):
- Disable profile selection for a pool (preferred): set pool override `runtime.profile.enabled=false` (fleet config) and restart the pool.
- Force baseline profile: `RUNTIME_PROFILE_NAME=baseline` (or pool config equivalent)
- Force “safe” mode: `RUNTIME_PROFILE_MODE=compat` (if supported) -> reduced batching, conservative KV
- Reduce scheduler pressure: `scheduler.max_inflight_requests`, `scheduler.max_queue_depth`, disable continuous batching
- Cap KV cache budget: `kv_cache.max_bytes` or `kv_cache.target_utilization`

Benji Okafor: Minor: env var names in runtime are `REDWOOD_RUNTIME_PROFILE_ENABLED` and `REDWOOD_RUNTIME_PROFILE_NAME` (per loader PR). Mode is `REDWOOD_RUNTIME_PROFILE_VARIANT` (e.g., `balanced`, `throughput`, `safe`). Also we should instruct to prefer pool-level config over per-node env var if possible.

Noah Patel: For “disable profiles”, the safest is to keep loader on but pin profile to `baseline@<version>` so we don’t change other default normalization behavior. We saw config drift when fully disabling loader in older builds.

Vanessa Ortiz: For fleet action: oncall can request a pool override via #eng-infra-oncall if they don’t have perms. We should write: “if this is >P1, page Infra oncall to apply pool override” and include the snippet they can copy/paste.

Aisha Bello: Dashboards to check (suggest ordering):
1) “Inference SLO Overview” (p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate) filtered by pool + model
2) “Hardware Profiles: Selection + Overrides” (selected profile, override source, %traffic)
3) “Scheduler Health” (queue depth, backpressure, inflight, batch size hist)
4) “GPU / Memory” (HBM used, fragmentation proxy, OOM count, restarts)
5) “Tokens/sec + cost” (throughput, tokens/s/GPU)

Omar El-Sayed: Add explicit OOM triage notes:
- If OOM/restarts correlate with long-context workload bucket, first reduce KV cache budget (target utilization down) and/or cap `max_seq_len` for affected routes.
- If L40S: memory headroom is tight; we’ve seen fragmentation. Recommend switching to `l40s-safe` profile variant if available.

Sahana Iyer: Also add “Perf canary signal” check. Sometimes this isn’t a prod-only issue; canary gate might already be red. Oncall should check if the build was profile-enabled and which gate tripped.

Sean Gallagher: Good. Adding a “Step 0: verify active profile” section with commands.

Sean Gallagher: Proposed “verify effective config” steps:
A) Debug endpoint (preferred):
`curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/debug/runtime_config | jq .profile`
Expect to see:
- `selected_profile_name`
- `selected_profile_version`
- `override_source` (fleet_default / pool_override / env / route_override)
- key knob diffs (scheduler + kv_cache)

B) CLI dump tool (if installed):
`redwood-runtime config dump --effective --format json`
Attach output to incident.

Benji Okafor: +1. Add note: debug endpoint is guarded in prod; you might need to exec into pod and hit localhost. Example:
`kubectl exec -it <pod> -- curl -s localhost:8080/debug/runtime_config`

Kira Thompson: We should spell out what logs to collect. Oncall always asks “what do I paste”.

Aisha Bello: Logs/metrics checklist:
- runtime startup logs that print compatibility matrix (runtime version vs profile pack version)
- profile selection log line (it logs normalized GPU SKU string)
- scheduler warnings (backpressure, queue saturations)
- OOM killer / CUDA OOM traces
- any “fallback” events (e.g., profile fallback to baseline due to version mismatch)

Noah Patel: Include a section “Symptoms -> likely cause -> first action”. For example:
- p95 up, tokens/sec flat: fairness/prefill-decode settings; switch variant `balanced` -> `safe` and reduce max batch
- tokens/sec down, GPU util down: kernel autoselect mismatch; pin kernel heuristics off (if we have a flag) or fallback to baseline
- spikes during streaming only: check streaming queue + flush interval, and watch tail latency on `stream=true`

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add a quick table. Need the exact flag for kernel autoselect fallback.

Benji Okafor: Kernel heuristics flag is `REDWOOD_KERNEL_AUTOSELECT=0` (forces prior default path). For H100 we also have bucket tuning `REDWOOD_KERNEL_BUCKETS=h100_default` vs `h100_seq_bucket_v2` (names might change; oncall should prefer “disable autoselect” vs picking buckets).

Paula Mendes: For rollback criteria: specify when to disable profile vs when to just reduce batching. Something like:
- P0/P1: disable profile in affected pool immediately after capturing config dump + 5min metrics snapshot
- P2: try variant switch / reduce queue first, watch 10–15 min

Vanessa Ortiz: Also remind them to check if this is isolated to one region/pool. We had that EU-West B200 issue where disabling just that pool was enough.

Sean Gallagher: Adding “scope check”:
- filter by `pool`, `region`, `gpu_sku`, `model`, `streaming` in dashboards
- check % of traffic on new profile (if only 5%, canary-only)

OncallBot: Reminder: For Sev-1 incidents, use /incident start in #incidents and include: impacted pools, customer impact, mitigation applied, and links to dashboards.

Kira Thompson: Copy/paste snippet for incident channel would be nice too.

Sean Gallagher: Draft incident snippet:
"Issue: latency regression after hardware profile change
Scope: pool=<>, region=<>, gpu=<>, model=<>, profile=<name>@<ver>, override=<source>
Symptoms: p95 +__%, error rate __%, restarts __
Mitigation: <disabled profile / switched variant / capped KV> at <time>
Evidence: runtime_config dump link, dashboard links, relevant logs excerpt"

Aisha Bello: Dashboard link placeholders are fine but please include the panel names exactly so people can search in Grafana. I’ll DM you the exact strings once I confirm.

Omar El-Sayed: One more: if Private customer escalation comes in (Helm deploy), oncall should tell them the supported override path:
- set Helm values `runtime.profile.enabled` / `runtime.profile.name` (not random env vars)
- collect `values.yaml` + runtime startup log + config dump

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll convert this thread into the Confluence runbook section, but keeping the “quick steps” here too.

Sean Gallagher: Final checklist (v0):
1) Identify pool/region/GPU/model + confirm active profile via debug endpoint
2) Check dashboards: SLO overview -> profile selection -> scheduler health -> GPU/memory
3) Collect artifacts: effective config dump + startup compatibility log + error traces
4) Apply mitigation:
   - Sev-1: pin `baseline` (pool override) or disable profile enabled flag
   - Sev-2: switch variant to `safe` + reduce queue/batch + cap KV
   - If kernel mismatch suspected: `REDWOOD_KERNEL_AUTOSELECT=0`
5) Watch 10–15 min; if not recovering, rollback profile for pool and page Runtime (Scheduling + Kernels)

Paula Mendes: Looks good. Only tweak: put the rollback thresholds right above step 4 so it’s actionable.

Benji Okafor: +1 and add note: after mitigation, verify that `override_source` changed (pool_override) so we know it actually took.

Sean Gallagher: Done. I’ll incorporate and post a link once the doc is up.

Benji Okafor: Minor: env var names in runtime are `REDWOOD_RUNTIME_PROFILE_ENABLED` and `REDWOOD_RUNTIME_PROFILE_NAME` (per loader PR). Mode is `REDWOOD_RUNTIME_PROFILE_VARIANT` (e.g., `balanced`, `throughput`, `safe`). Also we should instruct to prefer pool-level config over per-node env var if possible.

Noah Patel: For “disable profiles”, the safest is to keep loader on but pin profile to `baseline@<version>` so we don’t change other default normalization behavior. We saw config drift when fully disabling loader in older builds.

Vanessa Ortiz: For fleet action: oncall can request a pool override via #eng-infra-oncall if they don’t have perms. We should write: “if this is >P1, page Infra oncall to apply pool override” and include the snippet they can copy/paste.

Aisha Bello: Dashboards to check (suggest ordering):
1) “Inference SLO Overview” (p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate) filtered by pool + model
2) “Hardware Profiles: Selection + Overrides” (selected profile, override source, %traffic)
3) “Scheduler Health” (queue depth, backpressure, inflight, batch size hist)
4) “GPU / Memory” (HBM used, fragmentation proxy, OOM count, restarts)
5) “Tokens/sec + cost” (throughput, tokens/s/GPU)

Omar El-Sayed: Add explicit OOM triage notes:
- If OOM/restarts correlate with long-context workload bucket, first reduce KV cache budget (target utilization down) and/or cap `max_seq_len` for affected routes.
- If L40S: memory headroom is tight; we’ve seen fragmentation. Recommend switching to `l40s-safe` profile variant if available.

Sahana Iyer: Also add “Perf canary signal” check. Sometimes this isn’t a prod-only issue; canary gate might already be red. Oncall should check if the build was profile-enabled and which gate tripped.

Sean Gallagher: Good. Adding a “Step 0: verify active profile” section with commands.

Sean Gallagher: Proposed “verify effective config” steps:
A) Debug endpoint (preferred):
`curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/debug/runtime_config | jq .profile`
Expect to see:
- `selected_profile_name`
- `selected_profile_version`
- `override_source` (fleet_default / pool_override / env / route_override)
- key knob diffs (scheduler + kv_cache)

B) CLI dump tool (if installed):
`redwood-runtime config dump --effective --format json`
Attach output to incident.

Benji Okafor: +1. Add note: debug endpoint is guarded in prod; you might need to exec into pod and hit localhost. Example:
`kubectl exec -it <pod> -- curl -s localhost:8080/debug/runtime_config`

Kira Thompson: We should spell out what logs to collect. Oncall always asks “what do I paste”.

Aisha Bello: Logs/metrics checklist:
- runtime startup logs that print compatibility matrix (runtime version vs profile pack version)
- profile selection log line (it logs normalized GPU SKU string)
- scheduler warnings (backpressure, queue saturations)
- OOM killer / CUDA OOM traces
- any “fallback” events (e.g., profile fallback to baseline due to version mismatch)

Noah Patel: Include a section “Symptoms -> likely cause -> first action”. For example:
- p95 up, tokens/sec flat: fairness/prefill-decode settings; switch variant `balanced` -> `safe` and reduce max batch
- tokens/sec down, GPU util down: kernel autoselect mismatch; pin kernel heuristics off (if we have a flag) or fallback to baseline
- spikes during streaming only: check streaming queue + flush interval, and watch tail latency on `stream=true`

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add a quick table. Need the exact flag for kernel autoselect fallback.

Benji Okafor: Kernel heuristics flag is `REDWOOD_KERNEL_AUTOSELECT=0` (forces prior default path). For H100 we also have bucket tuning `REDWOOD_KERNEL_BUCKETS=h100_default` vs `h100_seq_bucket_v2` (names might change; oncall should prefer “disable autoselect” vs picking buckets).

Paula Mendes: For rollback criteria: specify when to disable profile vs when to just reduce batching. Something like:
- P0/P1: disable profile in affected pool immediately after capturing config dump + 5min metrics snapshot
- P2: try variant switch / reduce queue first, watch 10–15 min

Vanessa Ortiz: Also remind them to check if this is isolated to one region/pool. We had that EU-West B200 issue where disabling just that pool was enough.

Sean Gallagher: Adding “scope check”:
- filter by `pool`, `region`, `gpu_sku`, `model`, `streaming` in dashboards
- check % of traffic on new profile (if only 5%, canary-only)

OncallBot: Reminder: For Sev-1 incidents, use /incident start in #incidents and include: impacted pools, customer impact, mitigation applied, and links to dashboards.

Kira Thompson: Copy/paste snippet for incident channel would be nice too.

Sean Gallagher: Draft incident snippet:
"Issue: latency regression after hardware profile change
Scope: pool=<>, region=<>, gpu=<>, model=<>, profile=<name>@<ver>, override=<source>
Symptoms: p95 +__%, error rate __%, restarts __
Mitigation: <disabled profile / switched variant / capped KV> at <time>
Evidence: runtime_config dump link, dashboard links, relevant logs excerpt"

Aisha Bello: Dashboard link placeholders are fine but please include the panel names exactly so people can search in Grafana. I’ll DM you the exact strings once I confirm.

Omar El-Sayed: One more: if Private customer escalation comes in (Helm deploy), oncall should tell them the supported override path:
- set Helm values `runtime.profile.enabled` / `runtime.profile.name` (not random env vars)
- collect `values.yaml` + runtime startup log + config dump

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll convert this thread into the Confluence runbook section, but keeping the “quick steps” here too.

Sean Gallagher: Final checklist (v0):
1) Identify pool/region/GPU/model + confirm active profile via debug endpoint
2) Check dashboards: SLO overview -> profile selection -> scheduler health -> GPU/memory
3) Collect artifacts: effective config dump + startup compatibility log + error traces
4) Apply mitigation:
   - Sev-1: pin `baseline` (pool override) or disable profile enabled flag
   - Sev-2: switch variant to `safe` + reduce queue/batch + cap KV
   - If kernel mismatch suspected: `REDWOOD_KERNEL_AUTOSELECT=0`
5) Watch 10–15 min; if not recovering, rollback profile for pool and page Runtime (Scheduling + Kernels)

Paula Mendes: Looks good. Only tweak: put the rollback thresholds right above step 4 so it’s actionable.

Benji Okafor: +1 and add note: after mitigation, verify that `override_source` changed (pool_override) so we know it actually took.

Sean Gallagher: Done. I’ll incorporate and post a link once the doc is up.

source.messages:
oncall-bot: :rotating_light: PAGE FIRED - runtime/p95-latency > 800ms (count: 6)
pagerduty: Incident created (PD-2184) -> paging escalation to primary oncall
oncall-bot: Details: service=runtime model=redwood-v1 region=us-west2 window=3m p95=1820ms
Ava Chen: ack'd — I got the page, looking now :mag_right:
Noah Patel: ping — are we seeing any customer-facing errors? any 500s?
Ava Chen: quick check of console: error rate still normal, no 5xx spike. This looks like latency-only.
Ava Chen: dashboards -> https://grafana.internal/runtime/tdash?panel=latency&from=-5m link shows 3 short spikes, each ~20-40s
Mike R: I see corresponding small bursts of CPU on the GPU hosts around the same timestamps, maybe scheduling hiccups or short GC on sidecars
Ava Chen: rolling tail logs: `for pod in $(kubectl get pods -l app=runtime -o name); do kubectl logs $pod --since=5m | grep -i "evict\|gc\|out of memory"; done` — no OOM/gc messages returned
Noah Patel: any autoscaler activity? could be transient scale-up causing kernel warmups
Ava Chen: yes, autoscaler shows 2 brief scale events at +00:02 and +00:04 — new pods registered then deregistered after load drop
oncall-bot: PD-2184 acknowledged by Ava Chen
ops-bot: Alert flapping detected: 5 flaps in 10m, auto-resolve triggered for this page (reason: transient p95 spikes).
Ava Chen: confirmed auto-resolve from ops-bot. Current p95 back to ~120ms.
Mike R: marking as likely noisy alert due to short lived scale events + kernel / cold TCs. Not customer-facing.
Noah Patel: Can we suppress or add a cooldown on this rule? maybe require >2m sustained or sample count > X
Ava Chen: agree. I'll open a followup runbook item and lower sensitivity: create ticket INF-4527 to: adjust alert threshold to 900ms and require 3m sustained, add sample count > 10
Ava Chen: creating ticket now -> https://ph.internal/t/INF-4527
Noah Patel: also add a note to the oncall handoff doc so next shift knows this is noisy on us-west2 for now
Ava Chen: done. @Mike R can you keep an eye for repeat within the hour? If it flaps again we'll escalate to infra engineering
Mike R: will do. setting watch on Grafana and slack alert to #eng-oncall-notify
Ava Chen: handing off to Noah for the 10:30-11:30 window — brief summary: PD-2184 temporary p95 spikes due to autoscaler churn, auto-resolved, INF-4527 created to tune rule
Noah Patel: taking handoff. thanks! :thumbsup:
oncall-bot: handoff noted: new oncall=Noah Patel until 1764508400
Ava Chen: leaving note in incident channel and closing thread. If anyone sees recurrence, ping me or escalate to infra.
Noah Patel: thanks, all.

source.messages:
Peter Holtz: quick q for whoever’s around — seeing a bunch of new 429s in us-east canary. what are the *official* reason codes now, and which runbook section do we follow for overload vs quota? trying not to thrash.

incident-bot: (FYI) hot-route protection active: route_group=chat_completions tier=best_effort action=admission_control shed_rate=0.18 region=us-east

Owen Phillips: +1, Support pinging too. The response headers now include `x-redwood-429-reason` but I don’t remember the mapping.

Amara Diallo: hey — gateway side: we standardized on `x-redwood-429-reason` + consistent JSON error.

Amara Diallo: current values we’re emitting:
- `quota_exceeded` (hard quota window exhausted)
- `rate_limited` (per-second/minute limit hit; usually client burst)
- `overload_admission_control` (capacity protection kicking in; best-effort gets gated first)
- `overload_shed` (we accepted but then shed due to sustained pressure / queue depth)

Amara Diallo: also check `retry-after` (seconds) when present. we also set `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` for SDKs.

Peter Holtz: perfect. what’s the runbook split? overload vs quota path?

Rafael Mendes: overload path == anything starting with `overload_*`.

Rafael Mendes: Runbook is: "Runbook: overload protection and admission control".
- If `overload_admission_control`: start at section "Admission control active (best-effort gating)" (verify enterprise SLO stable, then confirm signals: queue_depth + scheduler_lag + gpu_saturation)
- If `overload_shed`: go to "Load shedding / request shedding" section (look at shed reason breakdown + whether a config change triggered it)

Rafael Mendes: quota path == `quota_exceeded` or `rate_limited`:
- `quota_exceeded`: follow "Quota evaluation" section + confirm tenant window counters (and whether they’re on the right tier)
- `rate_limited`: usually expected client-side; we just verify limit config and that it’s not accidentally applied to enterprise route_group

Aisha Bello: + for alerting/paging: we *don’t* page on best-effort `overload_admission_control` unless enterprise p95/p99 or 5xx burn is impacted. Pager should be driven by enterprise SLO burn alerts / protected routes.

Owen Phillips: is there a quick rule for “is this expected during pressure” vs “something broken”?

Rafael Mendes: heuristics:
- expected: best-effort 429s increase with `x-redwood-429-reason=overload_admission_control`, enterprise dashboards flat, no 5xx spike
- suspicious: enterprise tier getting `overload_*`, or 429 reason is `overload_shed` with rising 5xx + scheduler_lag climbing fast (possible cascade)

Amara Diallo: also FYI the body looks like:
`{"error":{"type":"rate_limit","code":"429","message":"Request throttled","reason":"overload_admission_control"}}`
(“reason” should match header)

Peter Holtz: amazing. can you drop the exact confluence link/section anchor? I’m on mobile.

Aisha Bello: confluence path: /eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-overload-protection-and-admission-control
anchors are roughly:
- #Admission-control-active
- #Load-shedding
- #Quota-vs-overload-quick-triage

Owen Phillips: :thumbsup: I’ll reply to Support with the mapping + “overload_* => overload runbook”

Peter Holtz: thanks all. closing loop on oncall notes — for current event, it’s best-effort gating only. will monitor enterprise burn + shed rate panels.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: if enterprise sees `overload_admission_control` at all, page SRE immediately + roll back allowlist / flag per runbook "Rollback criteria".

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: Comms Lead training Q&A thread. Drop questions here as you go through the deck/runbook. Biggest topic last session: what to do when root cause is unknown + we’re actively mitigating.

Christine Wong: 🙋‍♀️ When we’re in that awkward phase: dashboards red, we’re traffic shifting / rolling back, but we don’t know RC yet… do we post “Mitigating” or stay “Investigating” until we’re confident?

Wei Chen: +1. Also what do we *say* about mitigations without speculating / leaking details?

Rafael Mendes: Good question. Default guidance:
- Use **Investigating** until you can state a *fact* about what’s happening (symptom) and what we’re doing (action), without guessing cause.
- You can move to **Mitigating** once we’ve started a concrete mitigation and have evidence it’s helping OR it’s the primary active workstream.
- You do **not** need root cause to say “we’re applying mitigations.”

Miles Thornton: the thing we’re optimizing for is clarity + tempo, not perfect certainty. “Mitigating” can basically mean “we’re taking steps to reduce impact” even if RC unknown.

Christine Wong: ok, but what’s the line between “action” and “speculation”? e.g. we flipped fallback, or shifted traffic to another region.

Wei Chen: and do we mention “failover” or “capacity” publicly? sometimes those are sensitive.

Kira Thompson: on sensitivity: avoid anything that implies customer-specific capacity constraints or “we ran out of GPUs.” Better to frame as “traffic is being rerouted / we’re scaling capacity” without details.

Dev Patel: from Support side, customers mainly want: what’s broken, where, what’s the workaround, and when they’ll hear next. They do NOT need kernel-level explanations.

Rafael Mendes: yep. A good pattern when RC unknown:
1) symptom + component/region
2) mitigation *in progress* (what we’re doing, high-level)
3) workaround (if safe)
4) next update ETA

Allison Grant: also keep it consistent with signals. If it’s latency-only and errors are fine, don’t say “outage.” Say “degraded performance / elevated latency.”

Owen Phillips: can we get example copy blocks? it’s easier to follow with concrete text.

Rafael Mendes: example “Investigating” when RC unknown:
“Investigating: We’re investigating elevated error rates impacting **Hosted API → Streaming (us-east)**. We’ve engaged on-call engineers and are working to mitigate. Next update within 30 minutes or sooner.”

Rafael Mendes: example “Mitigating” without RC:
“Mitigating: We’ve identified a potential mitigation path and are actively applying changes to reduce impact to **Hosted API → Streaming (us-east)**. Some users may continue to see increased disconnects while changes roll out. Next update within 30 minutes or sooner.”

Christine Wong: that “potential mitigation path” line is nice. doesn’t overpromise.

Wei Chen: Should we ever say “we believe” / “likely” in public updates?

Rafael Mendes: sparingly. “We believe” is ok if it’s tied to an observable symptom (e.g., “we believe this is related to elevated upstream timeouts”) but avoid “likely hardware issue” / “probably provider” unless confirmed and ok’d.

Kira Thompson: + avoid naming vendors/providers in public comms unless Legal/Security has pre-approved wording.

Devon Price: incident-bot will help here. The draft templates intentionally avoid causal language and keep the mitigation text high-level. (PR-19344)

Rafael Mendes: then don’t. Don’t invent workarounds. You can say “We’ll share workarounds as we confirm them.”

Allison Grant: and if retries increase load, better to say “Some requests may fail; please use exponential backoff” only if SRE/IC agrees.

Wei Chen: approval flow question: if we’re posting Mitigating without RC, does it need Support approval?

Rafael Mendes: no approval needed for first Investigating. For Sev0/Sev1:
- Investigating: publish immediately.
- Identified/Mitigating: no formal approval required, but sanity check in #incidents if there’s time.
- Resolved: IC + Comms Lead must agree it’s actually stable; loop Support lead if there’s any ambiguity.

Kira Thompson: you can say “We’re rerouting traffic to reduce impact” but keep component/region status aligned to *experienced impact*. If us-east users are impacted, mark us-east degraded even if mitigation is routing elsewhere.

Wei Chen: question on wording: “partial outage” vs “degraded performance” when errors are spiky but not total.

Kira Thompson: depends. General rule: public status page can reflect “Dedicated” at a high level when there’s broad impact, but avoid customer-identifying details. For single-tenant isolated issues, we usually handle via Support/CS direct comms + post-incident summary if policy requires.

Christine Wong: this helps a lot. can we pin these example blocks somewhere?

Rafael Mendes: yep — I’ll add the exact examples to the Comms Lead section in the runbook + templates page and link back here. Keep questions coming as you run into edge cases.

Rafael Mendes: Comms Lead training Q&A thread. Drop questions here as you go through the deck/runbook. Biggest topic last session: what to do when root cause is unknown + we’re actively mitigating.

Christine Wong: 🙋‍♀️ When we’re in that awkward phase: dashboards red, we’re traffic shifting / rolling back, but we don’t know RC yet… do we post “Mitigating” or stay “Investigating” until we’re confident?

Wei Chen: +1. Also what do we *say* about mitigations without speculating / leaking details?

Rafael Mendes: Good question. Default guidance:
- Use **Investigating** until you can state a *fact* about what’s happening (symptom) and what we’re doing (action), without guessing cause.
- You can move to **Mitigating** once we’ve started a concrete mitigation and have evidence it’s helping OR it’s the primary active workstream.
- You do **not** need root cause to say “we’re applying mitigations.”

Miles Thornton: the thing we’re optimizing for is clarity + tempo, not perfect certainty. “Mitigating” can basically mean “we’re taking steps to reduce impact” even if RC unknown.

Christine Wong: ok, but what’s the line between “action” and “speculation”? e.g. we flipped fallback, or shifted traffic to another region.

Wei Chen: and do we mention “failover” or “capacity” publicly? sometimes those are sensitive.

Kira Thompson: on sensitivity: avoid anything that implies customer-specific capacity constraints or “we ran out of GPUs.” Better to frame as “traffic is being rerouted / we’re scaling capacity” without details.

Dev Patel: from Support side, customers mainly want: what’s broken, where, what’s the workaround, and when they’ll hear next. They do NOT need kernel-level explanations.

Rafael Mendes: yep. A good pattern when RC unknown:
1) symptom + component/region
2) mitigation *in progress* (what we’re doing, high-level)

Allison Grant: also keep it consistent with signals. If it’s latency-only and errors are fine, don’t say “outage.” Say “degraded performance / elevated latency.”

source.text:
Logan Wright: quick q on status page updates — what’s the approval flow supposed to be? when do we loop Support lead vs just IC signoff?

Rafael Mendes: good catch. current intent:
- Comms Lead drafts + posts
- IC is accountable for accuracy + approves anything beyond a basic “Investigating”
- Support lead looped for Sev0/Selv1 *if* we’re making customer-actionable statements or anything that could create ticket churn.

Christine Wong: +1. we’ve had a couple incidents where we posted “no customer impact” and Support immediately had 10 tickets saying otherwise :/ so for high sev, Support review is a sanity check on phrasing + scope.

Logan Wright: so does Support approval block TTFU? we’re trying to hit 15 min.

Miles Thornton: it shouldn’t. guideline we discussed in game day:
- first update (“Investigating…”) goes out fast, no approval gate, as long as it’s factual + scoped (component/region)
- any update that changes the claim materially (“Identified root cause”, “Mitigation in progress”, “Resolved”) → IC thumbs-up
- for Sev0/Sev1: ping Support lead in parallel, but don’t wait unless you’re about to say something that affects external comms expectations.

Dev Patel: from Support POV: please loop us when
1) you’re changing impacted components/regions (we need to update macros + customer replies)
2) you’re saying “Dedicated/Private unaffected” (customers will read that literally)
3) you’re offering timelines (even “should be stable in 10 min”)
If it’s just “Investigating elevated 5xx in Hosted API (us-east)” — post it.

Rafael Mendes: ^ exactly. and if Support isn’t immediately available, IC makes the call. we can’t hold updates hostage.

Logan Wright: ok makes sense. what about “Resolved” — do we need Support to approve that too?

Dev Patel: if it’s Sev0/Sev1, I’d prefer yes *when possible*, mainly because “Resolved” triggers a wave of “but I’m still seeing issues” tickets. If you include “Monitoring” step first, it helps a lot.

Christine Wong: agreed: use Monitoring before Resolved unless it’s super clear (rollback done, metrics recovered, no ongoing mitigation). monitoring copy sets expectations.

Rafael Mendes: writing this down as a simple rule for on-call:
- Investigating: Comms Lead can publish immediately (IC notified)
- Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved: IC approval required
- Sev0/Sev1: Support lead approval requested for Identified+Resolved, and for any customer-actionable language; don’t block the initial Investigating.

Logan Wright: perfect. is this documented somewhere? i can’t find it in the runbook.

Christine Wong: not yet, updating the runbook this week. for now: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ONCALL/Status+Page+Update+Runbook (see “Approval + escalation” section — WIP)

incident-bot: Reminder (Sev1): time-to-first-update target is 15m. Assign a Comms Lead if not already assigned.

Miles Thornton: also: if IC is heads-down, Comms Lead can grab a quick “:thumbsup:” in thread or on Zoom and treat that as approval. no need for a formal doc.

Owen Phillips: +1. and if you’re unsure whether a phrase needs Support review, default to NOT saying it. keep it factual: what we see, what component/region, what we’re doing, next update ETA.

Logan Wright: got it. thanks all. I’ll follow that flow and we’ll pull into the runbook + incident-bot workflow.

source.messages:
Christine Wong: dropping some quick on-call feedback on the status-page update runbook. Used it twice this week (Sev1 + a noisy Sev2) and it’s still a bit too manual during the first 10 mins.

Christine Wong: main pain: runbook says “verify impact on dashboards” but the links are scattered + we end up hunting for the right region/capability panels. during Sev1, we lost ~6-8 mins just clicking around.

Rafael Mendes: yep, want that kind of feedback. can you call out the specific steps that felt highest friction?

Christine Wong: sure.
1) step: identify component(s) impacted → we have to translate internal service names (gateway, scheduler) to public components (Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings) manually.
2) step: confirm region(s) impacted → runbook points to “component health dashboards” but doesn’t deep link per region.
3) step: post first update → template exists but still a bunch of copy/paste + adding components in statuspage UI.

Christine Wong: also the “double check approval” note is vague. in Sev1, i wasn’t sure if i needed Support lead before the first Investigating update or only once we mark Partial Outage.

Owen Phillips: +1 on approval ambiguity. in the moment i default to “ask in #support” and then we’re waiting on someone to respond.

Rafael Mendes: on approval: expectation is first Investigating update should not be blocked, esp for Sev1/Sev0. approvals apply when we’re changing states aggressively (e.g. resolved) or when the message includes customer-impact quantification that might be wrong. we should encode that more clearly.

Miles Thornton: for the “verify impact” step: could we have the runbook call out a single canonical dashboard landing page that has filters (component_id, region, capability) so you don’t chase links?

Aisha Bello: we do have a landing page but it’s not obvious. right now it’s under Observability → “Status Components”. i can add a short link + pre-set views for top combos (us-east streaming, eu-west api, etc.).

Christine Wong: that’d help. the most common path is “us-east + streaming” or “us-east + hosted api”. if the runbook had those links, would shave off time.

1) pre-fills impacted components + region

3) posts + logs in #incidents

Incident-bot: (bot) FYI: statuspage integration is in progress. See ENG-4822 / PR-19344.

Mina Haddad: on the mapping issue: it’s not just service→component, it’s capability split too. e.g. “chat completions OK but streaming disconnects spike” should hit Hosted API Streaming (us-east), not Hosted API (us-east). we need a quick reference in the runbook.

Aisha Bello: i can update dashboards to include a “recommended component” callout (based on labels) but we should be careful about false positives.

- (1) pick component(s) + region(s) (use incident intake fields)

Owen Phillips: + add a “minimum viable investigating” template. on-call spends too long trying to write perfect copy.

Christine Wong: yes. template should explicitly say it’s OK to be vague early, e.g. “We’re investigating elevated errors impacting Streaming in us-east. Next update in 30 minutes or sooner.”

Rafael Mendes: agreed. we want consistent, not overly specific.

Allison Grant: can we add an “anti-flap” note to runbook fast path? we shouldn’t mark degraded if it’s a single 2-min blip, but during Sev1 it’s obvious. guidance right now is too generic.

Mina Haddad: maybe: if burn-rate alert fired OR sustained error% > threshold for 5 mins → ok to set degraded. otherwise use “investigating” without changing component state? (or keep component “operational” but add incident?)

Christine Wong: +1. i’m also confused by “incident without component impact” vs “component degraded.” we should just have one clear recommendation.

Rafael Mendes: action items i’m capturing:

- add a mapping cheat sheet (service/capability → public component)
- incident-bot: command/flow to generate+publish investigating update w/ prefilled component+region

Aisha Bello: i’ll take dashboard landing + deep links. will post a shortlink in-thread once it’s ready.

Devon Price: i’ll sync w/ Rafael on bot flow. we can likely ship “draft text + components” first, then “one-click publish” after we’re confident in API retries/permissions.

Christine Wong: thanks. if we can shave even 5 mins off the first update, support tickets drop noticeably.

Owen Phillips: also add a tiny section: “if customers dispute status accuracy, do X” with a link to the escalation playbook. on-call ends up answering DMs otherwise.

Rafael Mendes: good call. will link escalation playbook + a one-liner “route to Support/CS; on-call provides dashboard evidence, not bespoke customer comms.”

Allison Grant: if you want, i can review the revised runbook text for clarity and consistency w/ signal standards.

Rafael Mendes: please do. i’ll post draft in Confluence and tag you + Christine for last-mile feedback.
